## **Instance-Conditioned GAN**

## **Arantxa Casanova**

Facebook AI Research École Polytechnique de Montréal Mila, Quebec AI Institute

**Marlène Careil Jakob Verbeek** Facebook AI Research Facebook AI Research Télécom Paris

**Michał Dro˙zd˙zal** _[⇤]_ **Adriana Romero-Soriano** _[⇤]_ Facebook AI Research Facebook AI Research McGill University

## **Abstract**

Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) can generate near photo realistic images in narrow domains such as human faces. Yet, modeling complex distributions of datasets such as ImageNet and COCO-Stuff remains challenging in unconditional settings. In this paper, we take inspiration from kernel density estimation techniques and introduce a non-parametric approach to modeling distributions of complex datasets. We partition the data manifold into a mixture of overlapping neighborhoods described by a datapoint and its nearest neighbors, and introduce a model, called instance-conditioned GAN (IC-GAN), which learns the distribution around each datapoint. Experimental results on ImageNet and COCO-Stuff show that IC-GAN significantly improves over unconditional models and unsupervised data partitioning baselines. Moreover, we show that IC-GAN can effortlessly transfer to datasets not seen during training by simply changing the conditioning instances, and still generate realistic images. Finally, we extend IC-GAN to the class-conditional case and show semantically controllable generation and competitive quantitative results on ImageNet; while improving over BigGAN on ImageNet-LT. Code and trained models to reproduce the reported results are available at `https://github.com/facebookresearch/ic_gan` .

## **1 Introduction**

Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) [18] have shown impressive results in unconditional image generation [27, 29]. Despite their success, GANs present optimization difficulties and can suffer from mode collapse, resulting in the generator not being able to obtain a good distribution coverage, and often producing poor quality and/or low diversity generated samples. Although many approaches attempt to mitigate this problem – _e.g._ [20, 32, 35, 38] –, complex data distributions such as the one in ImageNet [45] remain a challenge for unconditional GANs [33, 36]. Classconditional GANs [5, 39, 40, 56] ease the task of learning the data distribution by conditioning on class labels, effectively partitioning the data. Although they provide higher quality samples than their unconditional counterparts, they require labelled data, which may be unavailable or costly to obtain.

Several recent approaches explore the use of unsupervised data partitioning to improve GANs [2, 14, 17, 23, 33, 42]. While these methods are promising and yield visually appealing samples, their quality is still far from those obtained with class-conditional GANs. These methods make use of relatively coarse and non-overlapping data partitions, which oftentimes contain data points from different types of objects or scenes. This diversity of data points may result in a manifold with low

In this work, we introduce a new approach, called instance-conditioned GAN (IC-GAN), which extends the GAN framework to model a mixture of local data densities. More precisely, IC-GAN learns to model the distribution of the neighborhood of a data point, also referred to as _instance_ , by providing a representation of the instance as an additional input to both the generator and discriminator, and by using the neighbors of the instance as _real_ samples for the discriminator. By choosing a sufficiently large neighborhood around the conditioning instance, we avoid the pitfall

2

of excessively partitioning the data into small clusters. Given the overlapping nature of these clusters, increasing the number of partitions does not come at the expense of having less samples in each of them. Moreover, unlike when conditioning on discrete cluster indices, conditioning on instance representations naturally leads the generator to produce similar samples for similar instances. Interestingly, once trained, our IC-GAN can be used to effortlessly transfer to other datasets not seen during training by simply swapping-out the conditioning instances at inference time.

IC-GAN bears similarities with kernel density estimation (KDE), a non-parametric density estimator in the form of a mixture of parametrized kernels modeling the density around each training data point – see _e.g._ [4]. Similar to KDE, IC-GAN can be seen as a mixture density estimator, where each component is obtained by conditioning on a training instance. Unlike KDE, however, we do not model the data likelihood explicitly, but take an adversarial approach in which we model the local density implicitly with a neural network that takes as input the conditioning instance as well as a noise vector. Therefore, the _kernel_ in IC-GAN is no longer independent on the data point on which we condition, and instead of a kernel bandwidth parameter, we control the smoothness by choosing the neighborhood size of an instance from which we sample the _real_ samples to be fed to the discriminator.

We validate our approach on two image generation tasks: (1) _unlabeled_ image generation where there is no class information available, and (2) _class-conditional_ image generation. For the unlabeled scenario, we report results on the ImageNet and COCO-Stuff datasets. We show that IC-GAN outperforms previous approaches in unlabeled image generation on both datasets. Additionally, we perform a series of transfer experiments and demonstrate that an IC-GAN trained on ImageNet achieves better generation quality and diversity when testing on COCO-Stuff than the same model trained on COCO-Stuff. In the class-conditional setting, we show that IC-GAN can generate images with controllable semantics – by adapting both class and instance–, while achieving competitive sample quality and diversity on the ImageNet dataset. Finally, we test IC-GAN in ImageNetLT, a long-tail class distribution ablated version of ImageNet, highlighting the benefits of nonparametric density estimation in datasets with unbalanced classes. Figure 1 shows IC-GAN unlabeled ImageNet generations (a), IC-GAN class-conditional ImageNet generations (b), and IC-GAN transfer generations both in the unlabeled (c) and controllable class-conditional (d) setting.

## **3 Experimental evaluation**

We describe our experimental setup in Section 3.1, followed by results presented in the unlabeled setting in Section 3.2, dataset transfer in Section 3.3 and class-conditional generation in Section 3.4. We analyze the impact of the number of stored instances and neighborhood size in Section 3.5.

## **3.1 Experimental setup**

**Datasets.** We evaluate our model in the unlabeled scenario on ImageNet [45] and COCO-Stuff [6]. The ImageNet dataset contains 1.2M and 50k images for training and evaluation, respectively. COCOStuff is a very diverse and complex dataset which contains multi-object images and has been widely used for complex scene generation. We use the train and evaluation splits of [8], and the (un)seen subsets of the evaluation images with only class combinations that have (not) been seen during training. These splits contain 76k, 2k, 675 and 1.3k images, respectively. For the class-conditional image generation, we use ImageNet as well as ImageNet-LT [34]. The latter is a long-tail variant of ImageNet that contains a subset of 115k samples, where the 1,000 classes have between 5 and 1,280 samples each. Moreover, we use some samples of four additional datasets to highlight the transfer abilities of IC-GAN: Cityscapes [10], MetFaces [28], PACS [31] and Sketches [15].

**Evaluation protocol.** We report Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) [22], Inception Score (IS) [47], and LPIPS [57]. LPIPS computes the distance between the AlexNet activations of two images generated with two different latent vectors and same conditioning. On ImageNet, we follow [5], and

4

compute FID over 50k generated images and the 50k real validation samples are used as reference. On COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT, we compute the FID for each of the splits using all images in the split as reference, and sample the same number images. Additionally, in ImageNet-LT we stratify the FID by grouping classes based on the number of train samples: more than 100 (many-shot FID), between 20 and 100 (med-shot FID), and less than 20 (few-shot FID). For the reference set, we split the validation images along these three groups of classes, and generate a matching number of samples per group. In order to compute all above-mentioned metrics, IC-GAN requires instance features for sampling. Unless stated otherwise, we store 1,000 training set instances by applying k-means clustering to the training set and selecting the features of the data point that is the closest to each one of the centroids. All quantitative metrics for IC-GAN are reported over five random seeds for the input noise when sampling from the model.

**Network architectures and hyperparameters.** As feature extractor _fφ_ , we use a ResNet50 [21] trained in a self-supervised way with SwAV [7] for the unlabeled scenario; for the class-conditional IC-GAN, we use a ResNet50 trained for the classification task on either ImageNet or ImageNetLT [26]. For ImageNet experiments, we use BigGAN [5] as a baseline architecture, given its superior image quality and ubiquitous use in conditional image generation. For IC-GAN, we replace the class embedding layers in the generator by a fully connected layer that takes the instance features as input and reduces its dimensionality from 2,048 to 512; the same approach is followed to adapt the discriminator. For COCO-Stuff, we additionally include the state-of-the-art unconditional StyleGAN2 architecture [29], as it has shown good generation quality and diversity in the lower data regime [28, 29]. We follow its class-conditional version [28] to extend it to IC-GAN by replacing the input class embedding by the instance features. Unless stated otherwise, we set the size of the neighborhoods to _k_ =50 for ImageNet and _k_ =5 for both COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT. See the supplementary material for details on the architecture and optimization hyperparameters.

## **3.2 Unlabeled setting**

**ImageNet.** We start by comparing IC-GAN against previous work in Table 1. Note that unconditional BigGAN baseline is trained by setting all labels in the training set to zero, following [36, 42]. IC-GAN surpasses all previous approaches at both 64 _⇥_ 64 and 128 _⇥_ 128 resolutions in both FID and IS scores. At 256 _⇥_ 256 resolution, IC-GAN outperforms the concurrent unconditional diffusion-based model of [12]; the only other result we are aware of in this setting. Additional results in terms of precision and recall can be found in Table 8 in the supplementary material.

As shown in Figure 1a, IC-GAN generates high quality images preserving most of the appearance of the conditioning instance. Note that generated images are not mere training memorizations; as shown in the supplementary material, generated images differ substantially from the nearest training samples.

In this setup, no COCO-Stuff images are used to train the model, thus, all splits contain unseen objects combinations. Perhaps surprisingly, IC-GAN trained on ImageNet outperforms the same model trained on COCO-Stuff for all splits: 8.5 _vs._ 16.8 train FID at 128 resolution. This raises the question of how close ImageNet and COCO-Stuff data distributions are. We compute the FID between real data train split of the two datasets at 128 _⇥_ 128 resolution and obtain a score of 37 _._ 2. Hence, the remarkable transfer capabilities of IC-GAN are not explained by dataset similarity and may be attributed to the effectiveness of the ImageNet pre-trained feature

6

extractor and generator. When we replace the conditioning instances from COCO-Stuff with those of ImageNet, we obtain a train FID score of 43.5, underlining the important distribution shift that can be implemented by changing the conditioning instances.

Interestingly, the transferred IC-GAN also outperforms LostGANv2 and OC-GAN which condition on labeled bounding box annotations. Transferring the model from ImageNet boosts diversity w.r.t. the model trained on COCO-Stuff (see LPIPS in Table 2), which may be in part due to the larger _k_ =50 used for ImageNet training, compared to _k_ =5 when training on COCO-Stuff. Qualitative results of COCO-Stuff generations from the ImageNet pre-trained IC-GAN can be found in Figure 1c (top row) and Figure 3d. These generations suggest that IC-GAN is able to effectively leverage the large scale training on ImageNet to improve the quality and diversity of the COCO-Stuff scene generation, which contains significantly less data to train.

We further explore how the ImageNet trained IC-GAN transfers to conditioning on other datasets using Cityscapes, MetFaces, and PACS in Figure 1c. Generated images still preserve the semantics and style of the images for all datasets, although degrading their quality when compared to samples in Figure 1a, as the instances in these datasets –in particular MetFaces and PACS– are very different from the ImageNet ones. See Section F in the supplementary material for more discussion, additional evaluations, and more qualitative examples of dataset transfer.

## **3.4 Class-conditional setting**

**ImageNet.** In Table 3, we show that the class-conditioned IC-GAN outperforms BigGAN in terms of both FID and IS across all resolutions except the FID at 128 _⇥_ 128 resolution. It is worth mentioning that, unlike BigGAN, IC-GAN can control the semantics of the generated images by either fixing the instance features and swapping the class conditioning, or by fixing the class conditioning and swapping the instance features; see Figure 1b. As shown in the figure, generated images preserve semantics of both the class label and the instance, generating different dog breeds on similar backgrounds, or generating camels in the snow, an unseen scenario in ImageNet to the best of our knowledge. Moreover, in

Figure 1d, we show the transfer capabilities of our class-conditional IC-GAN trained on ImageNet and conditioned on instances from other datasets, generating camels in the grass, zebras in the city, and husky dogs with the style of MetFaces and PACS instances. These controllable conditionings enable the generation of images that are not present or very rare in the ImageNet dataset, _e.g._ camels surrounded by snow or zebras in the city. Additional qualitative transfer results which either fix the class label and swap the instance features, or vice-versa, can be found in Section F of the supplementary material.

**ImageNet-LT.** Due to the class imbalance in ImageNet-LT, selecting a subset of instances with either k-means or uniform sampling can easily result in ignoring rare classes, and penalizing their generation. Therefore, for this dataset we use all available 115k training instances to sample from the model and compute the metrics. In Table 4 we compare to BigGAN, showing that IC-GAN is better in terms of FID and IS for modeling this long-tailed distribution. Note that the improvement is noticeable for each of the three groups of classes with different number of samples, see many/med/few column. ## **3.5 Selection of stored instances and neighborhood size**

In this section, we empirically justify the k-means procedure to select the instances to sample from the model, consider the effect of the number of instances used to sample from the model, as well as the effect of the size _k_ of the neighborhoods _Ai_ used during training. The impact of different choices for the instance embedding function _fφ_ ( **x** ) is evaluated in the supplementary material.

**Selecting instances to sample from the model.** In Figure 4 (left), we compare two instance selection methods in terms of FID: uniform sampling (Random) and k-means (Clustered), where we select the closest instance to each cluster centroid, using _k_ = 50 neighbors during training (solid and dotted green lines). Random selection is consistently outperformed by k-means; selecting only 1,000 instances with k-means results in better FID than randomly selecting 5,000 instances. Moreover, storing more than 1,000 instances selected with k-means does not result in noticeable improvements in FID. Additionally, we computed FID metrics for the 1,000 ground truth images that are closest to the k-means cluster centers, obtaining 41 _._ 8 _±_ 0 _._ 2 FID, which is considerably higher than the 10 _._ 4 _±_ 0 _._ 1 FID we obtain with IC-GAN ( _k_ = 50) when using the same 1,000 cluster centers. This supports the idea that IC-GAN is generating data points that go beyond the stored instances, better recovering the data distribution.

We consider precision (P) and recall (R) [30] (using an InceptionV3 [51] as feature extractor and sampling 10,000 generated and real images) to disentangle the factors driving the improvement in FID, namely image quality and diversity (coverage) – see Figure 4 (right). We see that augmenting the number of stored instances results in slightly worse precision (image quality) but notably better recall (coverage). Intuitively, this suggests that by increasing the number of stored instances, we can better recover the data density at the expense of slightly degraded image quality in lower density regions of the manifold – see _e.g._ [11].
Figure 4: Impact on the number of stored instances used to evaluate IC-GAN and the size of the neighborhood _k_ . Experiments performed on the 64 _⇥_ 64 unlabeled ImageNet dataset.

8

**Neighborhood size.** In Figure 4 (both panels) we analyze the interplay between the neighborhood size and the number of instances used to recover the data distribution. For small numbers of stored instances, we observe that larger the neighborhoods lead to better (lower) FID scores (left-hand side of left panel). For recall, we also observe improvements for large neighborhoods when storing few instances (left-hand side of right panel), suggesting that larger neighborhoods are more effective in recovering the data distribution from few instances. This trend is reverted for large numbers of stored instances, where smaller values of _k_ are more effective. This supports the idea that the neighborhood size acts as a bandwidth parameter – similar to KDE –, that controls the smoothness of the implicitly learnt conditional distributions around instances. For example, _k_ = 500 leads to smoother conditional distributions than _k_ = 5, and as a result requires fewer stored instances to recover the data distribution. Moreover, as expected, we notice that the value of _k_ does not significantly affect precision (right panel). Overall, _k_ = 50 offers a good compromise, exhibiting top performance across all metrics when using at least 500 stored instances. We visualize the smoothness effect by means of a qualitative comparison across samples from different neighborhood sizes in Section K of the supplementary material. Using (very) small neighborhoods ( _e.g._ of _k_ = 5), results in lower diversity in the generated images.

## **4 Related work**

**Data partitioning for GANs.** Previous works have attempted to improve the image generation quality and diversity of GANs by partitioning the data manifold through clustering techniques [2, 19, 33, 36, 42, 46], or by leveraging mixture models in their design [14, 17, 23]. In particular, [36, 46] apply k-means on representations from a pre-trained feature extractor to cluster the data, and then use cluster indices to condition the generator network. Then, [19, 33] introduce an alternating two-stage approach where the first stage applies k-means to the discriminator feature space and the second stage trains a GAN conditioned on the cluster indices. Similarly, [42] proposes to train a clustering network, which outputs pseudolabels, in cooperation with the generator. Further, [2] trains a feature extractor with self-supervised pre-training tasks, and creates a k-nearest neighbor graph in the learned representation space to cluster connected points into the same sub-manifold. In this case, a different generator is then trained for each identified sub-manifold. By contrast, IC-GAN uses fine-grained overlapping data neighborhoods in tandem with conditioning on rich feature embeddings (instances) to learn a localized distribution around each data point.

**Mitigating mode collapse in GANs.** Works which attempt to mitigate mode collapse may also bear some similarities to ours. In [32], the discriminator takes into consideration multiple random samples from the same class to output a decision. In [35], a mixed batch of generated and real samples is fed to the discriminator with the goal of predicting the ratio of real samples in the batch. Other works use a mixture of generators [17, 23] and encourage each generator to focus on generating samples from a different mode. Similarly, in [14], the discriminator is pushed to form clusters in its representation space, where each cluster is represented by a Gaussian kernel. In turn, the generator tends to learn to generate samples covering all clusters, hence mitigating mode collapse. By contrast, we focus on discriminating between real and generated _neighbors_ of an instance conditioning, by using a single generator network trained following the GAN formulation.

**Conditioning on feature vectors** . Very recent work [37] uses image self-supervised feature representations to condition a generative model whose objective is to produce a good input reconstruction; this requires storing the features of all training samples. In contrast, our objective is to learn a localized distribution (as captured by nearest neighboring images) around each conditioning instance, and we only need to save a very small subset of the dataset features to approximately recover the training distribution.

**Kernel density estimation and adversarial training.** Connections between adversarial training and nonparametric density estimation have been made in prior work [1]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no prior work models the dataset density in a nonparametric fashion with a localized distribution around each data point with a single conditional generation network.

**Complex scene generation.** Existing methods for complex scene generation, where natural looking scenes contain multiple objects, most often aim at controllability and rely on detailed conditionings such as a scene graphs [3, 25], bounding box layouts [48–50, 58], semantic segmentation masks [9, 43, 44, 52, 55] or more recently, freehand sketches [16]. All these methods leverage intricate pipelines to generate complex scenes and require labeled datasets. By contrast, our approach

9

relies on instance conditionings which control the global semantics of the generation process, and does not require any dataset labels. It is worth noting that complex scene generation is often characterized by unbalanced, strongly long tailed datasets. Long-tail class distributions negatively affect classconditional GANs, as they struggle to generate visually appealing samples for classes in the tail [8]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no other previous work tackles this problem for GANs.

## **5 Discussion**

**Contributions.** We presented instance-conditioned GAN (IC-GAN), which models dataset distributions in a non-parametric way by conditioning both generator and discriminator on instance features. We validated our approach on the unlabeled setting, showing consistent improvements over baselines on ImageNet and COCO-Stuff. Moreover, we showed through transfer experiments, where we condition the ImageNet-trained model on instances of other datasets, the ability of IC-GAN to produce compelling samples from different data distributions. Finally, we validated IC-GAN in the class-conditional setting, obtaining competitive results on ImageNet and surpassing the BigGAN baseline on the challenging ImageNet-LT; and showed compelling controllable generations by swapping the class-conditioning given a fixed instance or the instance given a fixed conditioning.

**Limitations.** IC-GAN showed excellent image quality for labeled (class-conditional) and unlabeled image generation. However, as any machine learning tool, it has some limitations. First, as kernel density estimator approaches, IC-GAN requires storing training instances to use the model. Experimentally, we noticed that for complex datasets, such as ImageNet, using 1,000 instances is enough to approximately cover the dataset distribution. Second, the instance feature vectors used to condition the model are obtained with a pre-trained feature extractor (self-supervised in the unlabeled case) and depend on it. We speculate that this limitation might be mitigated if the feature extractor and the generator are trained jointly, and leave it as future work. Third, although, we highlighted excellent transfer potential of our approach to unseen datasets, we observed that, in the case of transfer to datasets that are _very_ different from ImageNet, the quality of generated images degrades.

**Broader impacts.** IC-GAN brings with it several benefits such as excellent image quality in labeled (class-conditional) and unlabeled image generation tasks, and the transfer potential to unseen datasets, enabling the use of our model on a variety of datasets without the need of fine-tuning or re-training. Moreover, in the case of class-conditional image generation, IC-GAN enables controllable generation of content by adapting either the style – by changing the instance – or the semantics – by altering the class –. Thus, we expect that our model can positively affect the workflow for creative content generators. That being said, with improving image quality in generative modeling, there is some potential for misuse. A common example are _deepfakes_ , where a generative model is used to manipulate images or videos well enough that humans cannot distinguish real from fake, with the intent to misinform. We believe, however, that open research on generative image models also contributes to better understand such synthetic content, and to detect it where it is undesirable. Recently, the community has also started to undertake explicit efforts towards detecting manipulated content by organizing challenges such as the Deepfake Detection Challenge [13].

## **References**

- [1] M Ehsan Abbasnejad, Qinfeng Shi, Anton van den Hengel, and Lingqiao Liu. A generative adversarial density estimator. In _CVPR_ , 2019.
- [2] Mohammadreza Armandpour, Ali Sadeghian, Chunyuan Li, and Mingyuan Zhou. Partition-guided GANs. _arXiv preprint_ , (arXiv:2104.00816), 2021.
- [3] Oron Ashual and Lior Wolf. Specifying object attributes and relations in interactive scene generation. In _CVPR_ , 2019.

- [4] C. Bishop. _Pattern recognition and machine learning_ . Spinger-Verlag, 2006.

- [5] Andrew Brock, Jeff Donahue, and Karen Simonyan. Large scale GAN training for high fidelity natural image synthesis. In _ICLR_ . URL `https://openreview.net/forum?id=B1xsqj09Fm` .

- [6] Holger Caesar, Jasper Uijlings, and Vittorio Ferrari. COCO-Stuff: Thing and stuff classes in context. In _CVPR_ , 2018.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Instance-Conditioned GAN (IC-GAN), a framework that models complex data distributions by conditioning generators and discriminators on instance-specific feature neighborhoods. By treating instances as local density anchors, the method achieves strong performance on ImageNet and COCO-Stuff, including impressive transfer capabilities across domains.

IC-GAN provides a clever, non-parametric approach to GAN conditioning that effectively bypasses the rigidity of class-label constraints. The empirical results are strong, particularly in the challenging unlabeled and long-tail regimes, making this a significant contribution to generative modeling.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity to Feature Extractor Bias: The methodology relies heavily on the quality and domain-specificity of the pre-trained feature extractor f_phi (e.g., SwAV ResNet50). Section 3.2 notes that performance degrades when transferring to datasets like MetFaces or PACS, which the authors attribute to distribution shifts. However, the paper does not systematically analyze how the choice of feature space (e.g., supervised vs. self-supervised) interacts with the neighborhood definition in Equation 1. A more rigorous ablation comparing different feature backbones would clarify whether the observed performance is a property of the IC-GAN architecture or simply a reflection of the pre-trained features' semantic density.

- Ambiguity in Neighborhood Density Modeling: The paper posits that IC-GAN models a mixture of local densities, yet the neighborhood size k is treated as a hyperparameter rather than an adaptive density estimator. In Section 3.5, the authors observe that larger neighborhoods behave like KDE bandwidths, but they do not provide a mechanism to determine the optimal k for different regions of the data manifold. If the density of the feature space is non-uniform, a fixed k may lead to over-smoothing in sparse regions or under-sampling in dense regions. The authors should provide a more formal justification or an adaptive strategy for k to ensure the model remains robust across varying data densities.

- Computational and Memory Scaling: While the paper claims to be a non-parametric approach, it effectively requires storing a subset of instances for inference and training. Section 3.5 mentions that 1,000 instances are sufficient for ImageNet, but it does not address how this scales to larger, more diverse datasets or real-time applications where memory is constrained. The reliance on k-means to select these instances introduces a dependency on the clustering algorithm's success in capturing the full data manifold. A more thorough discussion on the trade-off between the number of stored instances and the diversity of generated samples (beyond the brief mention in Figure 4) is necessary to understand the method's practical limits.

- Lack of Joint Training Analysis: The authors acknowledge in the discussion that the feature extractor and the generator are trained separately, suggesting that joint training could mitigate current limitations. However, the paper lacks a preliminary experiment or theoretical discussion on the potential instability of joint training. Without such an analysis, it is difficult to determine if the current separation is a design choice or a limitation of the proposed adversarial objective. Including a small-scale experiment or a more detailed discussion on the gradients flowing back to the feature extractor would greatly strengthen the paper's claims regarding future extensibility.

- Formal assessment of the neighborhood-density relationship: The paper treats the neighborhood size k as a global hyperparameter, yet it claims to model a mixture of local densities. Because the density of the feature space is inherently non-uniform, a fixed k likely leads to over-smoothing in sparse regions and under-sampling in dense ones. To demonstrate the robustness of the approach, the authors should compute the local density of the feature space for a standard dataset like ImageNet and show how the model's performance (FID) varies when k is adapted to the local density of the conditioning instance. This would clarify whether the 'implicit KDE' claim holds across the entire manifold or only in high-density regions.

- Ablation of feature extractor bias on transfer performance: The paper attributes the performance degradation during transfer to 'distribution shifts' between datasets. However, it is unclear if this is due to the IC-GAN architecture or the specific semantic bias of the pre-trained ResNet50 (SwAV). A controlled experiment is needed where the same IC-GAN architecture is trained using features from different backbones—specifically comparing a supervised ResNet50, a self-supervised SwAV ResNet50, and a CLIP-based encoder. This would isolate the contribution of the feature space to the transferability claim and determine if the model's success is a property of the conditioning mechanism or the quality of the underlying semantic embeddings.

- Computational scalability analysis for large-scale deployments: The paper claims the model is a non-parametric approach that avoids excessive partitioning, but it relies on an offline k-means selection of 1,000 instances. There is no discussion on how this selection process scales to datasets with significantly higher semantic complexity or larger sample sizes. The authors should provide a scaling analysis or a simulation showing the impact of the number of stored instances (M) versus the neighborhood size (k) on the FID/Precision/Recall metrics. This would provide the necessary guidance for practitioners on how to calibrate the memory-performance trade-off for datasets that are not as well-behaved as ImageNet.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Include a more comprehensive ablation study on the impact of different pre-trained feature extractors (e.g., comparing CLIP, SwAV, and supervised ResNet) to isolate the contribution of the IC-GAN framework from the feature backbone.

- Provide a more detailed analysis or a heuristic for selecting the neighborhood size k in regions of varying data density, moving beyond the current fixed-k approach.

- Expand the discussion on the memory-performance trade-off, specifically addressing how the number of stored instances affects the model's ability to cover long-tail distributions versus high-density modes.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Instance-Conditioned GAN (IC-GAN), a non-parametric approach to modeling complex data distributions by partitioning the data manifold into overlapping neighborhoods and learning the local distribution around each instance. The method shows strong empirical results on ImageNet and COCO-Stuff, with notable transfer capabilities and competitive performance in class-conditional settings.

IC-GAN presents a conceptually clean and well-executed extension of GANs to non-parametric density estimation. The empirical results are strong across multiple datasets and settings, and the transfer experiments are particularly compelling. The paper is clearly written and the method is well-motivated by the analogy to kernel density estimation.

Overview issues:

- Lack of theoretical justification for why instance conditioning improves training stability or sample diversity: The paper motivates IC-GAN by analogy to kernel density estimation, but provides no formal analysis of how instance conditioning affects GAN training dynamics, mode coverage, or convergence. The claim that overlapping neighborhoods avoid the pitfalls of small clusters (Section 1) is intuitive but unproven. A theoretical analysis—even a simplified one—showing that the instance-conditioned objective has better properties than standard unconditional GANs (e.g., lower variance gradients, better coverage) would significantly strengthen the contribution. Without it, the method remains an empirical heuristic whose success is not fully explained.

- Potential overfitting to stored instances and risk of memorization: The paper argues that generated images are not mere memorizations (Section 3.5), but the evidence is indirect: comparing FID of generated samples to FID of ground-truth cluster centers. This does not rule out the model memorizing and interpolating between stored instances, especially when the neighborhood size k is small (e.g., k=5 for COCO-Stuff). A more direct test would be to measure the nearest neighbor distance between generated samples and training instances, or to perform a membership inference attack. The risk is that IC-GAN's strong FID scores partly reflect memorization rather than generalization, which would undermine the claim of learning local distributions.

- Insufficient ablation studies to isolate the contribution of instance conditioning: The paper compares IC-GAN to several baselines, but the ablations are limited. Key design choices—such as the use of overlapping neighborhoods vs. non-overlapping clusters, the choice of feature extractor (SwAV vs. supervised), and the effect of conditioning the discriminator vs. only the generator—are not systematically ablated. For example, Table 1 shows that IC-GAN outperforms self-supervised GAN and unconditional BigGAN, but it is unclear how much of the gain comes from instance conditioning vs. the specific architecture or training details. An ablation that varies one component at a time (e.g., replacing instance conditioning with cluster indices, or removing discriminator conditioning) would clarify the method's essential ingredients.

- Risk of mode collapse being masked by instance memory: The paper reports strong recall (coverage) metrics, but these are computed using a fixed feature extractor (InceptionV3) that may not capture fine-grained modes. The instance memory (stored instances) could act as a crutch: the model might generate samples that are close to stored instances in the feature space but fail to cover the full data distribution. The analysis in Figure 4 shows that increasing the number of stored instances improves recall, but this could simply reflect better coverage of the stored instances themselves, not of the underlying data distribution. A more rigorous evaluation of mode coverage—e.g., counting the number of distinct modes generated, or using a more sensitive metric—would address this concern.

- Limited evaluation on diverse and high-resolution datasets: The paper evaluates on ImageNet (up to 256x256) and COCO-Stuff (up to 256x256), but does not test on higher-resolution datasets like FFHQ (1024x1024) or LSUN (e.g., bedrooms at 256x256). The method's scalability to high-resolution images is unclear, especially given the need to store and retrieve instance features. Additionally, the transfer experiments are limited to datasets that are somewhat similar to ImageNet (Cityscapes, MetFaces, PACS); testing on a truly out-of-distribution dataset (e.g., medical images, satellite imagery) would better demonstrate generalization. The paper claims that IC-GAN can 'effortlessly transfer' (Section 1), but this claim is not supported by experiments on diverse domains.

- Assumption that nearest neighbors in a pretrained feature space are semantically meaningful: The paper uses a SwAV-pretrained ResNet50 for feature extraction, assuming that cosine similarity in this space captures semantic similarity. While SwAV is a strong self-supervised method, this assumption may break down for datasets with very different visual characteristics (e.g., medical images, sketches). The paper does not analyze the quality of the neighborhoods (e.g., what fraction of neighbors share the same class or visual concept). A simple analysis—e.g., showing that neighbors are semantically coherent for a random subset of instances—would strengthen the motivation. Without it, the method's reliance on a fixed feature extractor is a potential weakness.

- Assumption that instance memory size and neighborhood size are dataset-independent: The paper sets k=50 for ImageNet and k=5 for COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT, with limited justification. The analysis in Section 3.5 shows that performance depends on both k and the number of stored instances, but does not provide clear guidelines for choosing these hyperparameters on a new dataset. The claim that k acts as a bandwidth parameter (Section 5) is intuitive, but the paper does not show how to select k in practice (e.g., via cross-validation or a heuristic). Practitioners would benefit from a more systematic analysis of the trade-offs and recommendations for setting these parameters.

- Assumption that the discriminator's conditioning on instances does not introduce unintended biases: The discriminator is conditioned on instance features, which could lead to shortcut learning: the discriminator might learn to distinguish real from fake based on low-level features of the conditioning instance rather than the generated sample's quality. This could degrade generation quality, especially when the instance features are noisy or uninformative. The paper does not analyze this risk or propose mitigations (e.g., gradient penalties, feature matching). An ablation that removes discriminator conditioning (while keeping generator conditioning) would help assess whether this is a concern.

- No formal comparison to non-overlapping cluster GANs: The paper claims that overlapping neighborhoods avoid the pitfall of excessively partitioning data into small clusters, but it never directly compares IC-GAN to a non-overlapping cluster-conditioned GAN under matched conditions. For example, one could replace the instance-conditioned generator with a cluster-index-conditioned generator using the same BigGAN backbone, the same number of clusters (e.g., 1,000), and the same feature extractor. Without this ablation, it is unclear whether the gains come from overlapping neighborhoods or simply from conditioning on a richer feature vector. Adding a column to Table 1 labeled 'Cluster-conditioned BigGAN (k-means, 1,000 clusters)' would settle this.

- No analysis of neighborhood semantic coherence: The method assumes that nearest neighbors in the SwAV feature space are semantically meaningful, but the paper provides no quantitative evidence for this assumption. For a random subset of 100 ImageNet instances, one could compute the fraction of neighbors that share the same ImageNet class label, or measure the average pairwise LPIPS distance within neighborhoods. If neighborhoods are semantically incoherent, the local density model may mix unrelated modes, which would undermine the KDE analogy. A simple table or histogram in Section 3.5 would address this.

- No guidance for choosing neighborhood size on a new dataset: The paper sets k=50 for ImageNet and k=5 for COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT, but provides no practical rule for selecting k on an unseen dataset. The analysis in Figure 4 shows that k interacts with the number of stored instances, but it does not derive a heuristic (e.g., k proportional to the square root of dataset size, or k set via cross-validated FID on a held-out split). Without such guidance, practitioners cannot apply IC-GAN reliably. A short paragraph in Section 3.5 or the supplementary material recommending a starting value and a tuning procedure would fill this gap.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a theoretical analysis or intuition for why instance conditioning improves GAN training stability and mode coverage, even if simplified.

- Include a direct test for memorization (e.g., nearest neighbor distance between generated and training samples) and discuss the implications.

- Conduct ablation studies that isolate the effect of overlapping neighborhoods, discriminator conditioning, and the choice of feature extractor.

- Evaluate mode coverage more rigorously (e.g., counting distinct modes or using a more sensitive metric) to rule out masked mode collapse.

- Test on at least one higher-resolution dataset (e.g., FFHQ 256x256 or LSUN) and one truly out-of-distribution dataset to demonstrate scalability and generalization.

comments.title: Discriminator parameter notation mismatch
comments.quote: In IC-GAN, we adopt an adversarial approach to train the generator $G_{\theta_{G}}$. Therefore, our generator is jointly trained with a discriminator $D_{\theta_{G}}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{h}_{i})$ that discerns between real neighbors and generated neighbors of $\mathbf{h}_{i}$, as shown in Figure 2b.
comments.explanation: The discriminator is written as $D_{\theta_{G}}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{h}_{i})$, but the subscript $\theta_{G}$ is the same as the generator's parameter. The discriminator should have its own parameters, e.g., $\theta_{D}$. This appears to be a typographical error, as later in the same section the class-conditional extension correctly uses $D_{\theta_{D}}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{h}_{i},\mathbf{y}_{j})$. Rewrite the quoted text as "a discriminator $D_{\theta_{D}}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{h}_{i})$" to match the later notation and standard practice.

comments.title: Missing uncertainty for BigGAN† baseline

The footnote says '†: numbers from the original paper, as training diverged with the BigGAN opensourced code.' This is acceptable if the original paper did not report error bars, but the table should explicitly note that the BigGAN† values are point estimates without uncertainty, to avoid misleading comparisons. Add a footnote or a note in the caption stating that the BigGAN† values are taken from the original paper and do not include standard deviations.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about unseen scenarios
comments.quote: generating camels in the snow, an unseen scenario in ImageNet to the best of our knowledge.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'camels in the snow' is an unseen scenario in ImageNet is presented without evidence. The paper does not provide a systematic check of whether any ImageNet training images contain camels with snow backgrounds. While the authors state 'to the best of our knowledge,' this is a factual claim about the dataset that should be verifiable. A simple analysis — e.g., checking ImageNet class labels for 'camel' and inspecting a random sample of those images for snow — would substantiate the claim. Without such evidence, the claim remains anecdotal. Add a brief verification in the supplementary material or qualify the statement as 'qualitatively appears to be an unseen combination.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about mitigating feature extractor limitation
comments.quote: We speculate that this limitation might be mitigated if the feature extractor and the generator are trained jointly, and leave it as future work.
comments.explanation: The speculation that joint training of the feature extractor and generator would mitigate the limitation is not supported by any analysis or reference. Joint training could introduce new challenges (e.g., instability, mode collapse) and may not improve feature quality. The paper should either provide a brief justification (e.g., citing related work on end-to-end training) or remove the speculation. As written, it is an unsupported conjecture that may mislead readers about the feasibility of the proposed fix.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Instance-Conditioned GAN (IC-GAN), a non-parametric approach that partitions the data manifold into overlapping neighborhoods and conditions both generator and discriminator on instance features. The method draws inspiration from kernel density estimation and demonstrates strong results on ImageNet, COCO-Stuff, and class-conditional tasks. While the core idea is intuitive and the empirical results are promising, the paper contains several methodological gaps regarding training dynamics, retrieval overhead, and class-conditional design choices that need to be addressed before publication.

The paper presents a novel and conceptually clean way to improve GAN diversity and quality by conditioning on local data neighborhoods. The experimental validation across multiple datasets and resolutions is thorough, and the transfer learning experiments are particularly compelling. However, the methodology lacks critical details on implementation feasibility, and certain design choices in the class-conditional extension create fundamental training inconsistencies that weaken the theoretical and empirical claims.

Overview issues:

- Class-conditional neighborhood mismatch breaks training consistency: In Section 2, the class-conditional extension defines neighborhoods \(\mathcal{A}_i\) based on feature similarity, explicitly noting that neighbors often belong to different classes than the conditioning instance. The generator and discriminator are jointly conditioned on a specific class label \(\mathbf{y}_j\), yet the real samples fed to the discriminator are sampled from \(\mathcal{A}_i\), which may not share that class. This creates a distributional mismatch where the discriminator learns to distinguish real samples that might contradict the conditioning signal from generated samples that strictly follow it. This flaw likely forces the discriminator to rely on irrelevant features or causes the generator to ignore the class signal, directly impacting the reported class-conditional FID and IS scores. The authors should either restrict \(\mathcal{A}_i\) to same-class neighbors or explicitly analyze and mitigate this mismatch in the revision.

- Discriminator memorization risk with small neighborhood sizes: Equation 1 frames the discriminator's task as distinguishing between \(k\) real neighbor images and generated ones. With the reported settings (\(k=5\) for COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT, \(k=50\) for ImageNet), the real data pool per instance is extremely small. A standard discriminator can easily memorize these exact images, reducing the adversarial game to a trivial classification task and encouraging the generator to simply reproduce the neighbors rather than learning the underlying local density. The claim that generated images are not mere memorizations relies solely on visual inspection. The paper needs quantitative evidence, such as reporting discriminator accuracy on neighbor identification or comparing the generator's output distribution against the empirical neighbor distribution, to validate that the model learns a genuine density.

- Missing details on training-time retrieval and memory overhead: The methodology requires sampling real neighbors from \(\mathcal{A}_i\) during every training iteration. For ImageNet's 1.2 million images, computing or retrieving k-NN neighborhoods on the fly is computationally prohibitive. Section 3.1 mentions using a pre-trained ResNet for feature extraction but completely omits how the neighborhoods are stored, indexed, or retrieved during training. An approximate nearest neighbor index or a precomputed k-NN graph is necessary, but its construction cost, memory footprint, and impact on training throughput are not discussed. Without this information, it is difficult to assess the practical feasibility of the approach or reproduce the training pipeline, which is a critical omission for a methodology paper.

- Unexamined dependency on the pre-trained feature extractor: The entire neighborhood partitioning strategy depends on the feature extractor \(f_\phi\) and the cosine similarity metric to define meaningful local densities. The paper uses a fixed SwAV or classification ResNet without analyzing how sensitive the generation quality is to this choice. If the embedding space does not align with perceptual or semantic relevance, the neighborhoods will contain semantically mismatched images, degrading generation quality. The authors mention joint training as future work but do not provide an ablation showing how different extractors or distance metrics affect FID/IS or neighborhood coherence. Adding a sensitivity analysis or a brief ablation on the feature extractor choice would clarify the method's robustness and practical utility.

- Lack of analysis on training dynamics and stability: Conditioning on deterministic instance features rather than stochastic noise fundamentally alters the gradient flow and optimization landscape. The paper reports strong quantitative results in Section 3 but provides no analysis of training stability, gradient norms, or convergence behavior. Non-parametric conditioning can introduce sharp gradients or vanishing signals if the discriminator overfits to the fixed instances. The ablation in Section 3.5 and Figure 4 focuses on neighborhood size and instance selection but ignores how these choices affect training loss curves or stability across seeds. Including a discussion of training dynamics, such as gradient statistics or stability metrics, would strengthen the empirical claims and help practitioners implement the method reliably.

- Missing worked example comparing implicit density to KDE: The paper frames IC-GAN as a non-parametric mixture density estimator analogous to kernel density estimation (KDE), replacing the fixed bandwidth with a tunable neighborhood size k. However, the theoretical analogy remains ungrounded. The authors should compute a worked special case using a low-dimensional parametric model, such as a 2D Gaussian mixture, to show how the implicit density learned by IC-GAN converges to or diverges from the explicit KDE solution as k varies. This calculation would clarify the relationship between neighborhood size and effective bandwidth. Without this tractable example, the KDE analogy stays purely descriptive. It also leaves readers without a way to calibrate the method's behavior on simpler distributions.

- Missing guidance on feature extraction for out-of-distribution transfer: The transfer claim relies on swapping conditioning instances from unseen datasets into an ImageNet-trained model. The paper uses a fixed pre-trained feature extractor to compute instance features for these new datasets but provides no analysis of how the embedding space alignment affects transfer quality. If the cosine similarity metric was optimized only for ImageNet, it may fail to capture semantic proximity for out-of-domain data. This misalignment would cause the nearest neighbors to be semantically mismatched, directly degrading generation quality. The authors need to provide a concrete protocol or sensitivity analysis for handling the embedding space during transfer. Specifying whether the extractor should be fine-tuned or replaced would make the effortless transfer claim reproducible.

- Missing analysis of neighborhood composition in long-tail settings: The paper claims IC-GAN improves generation on ImageNet-LT by leveraging non-parametric density estimation, yet it does not explain how the method handles extreme class imbalance during neighborhood construction. With k neighbors sampled uniformly from the feature space, rare classes will inevitably pull neighbors from dominant classes if the embedding space is dominated by frequent categories. The authors should analyze the neighborhood class composition for tail classes to verify that the learned local densities remain coherent. An ablation showing how k affects tail-class generation quality versus memorization would substantiate the claim that the framework genuinely benefits long-tail distributions. This analysis is necessary because the current results only report aggregate metrics.

- Missing evaluation of learned density quality beyond generation metrics: The authors position IC-GAN as a non-parametric density estimator but evaluate it exclusively through downstream generation metrics like FID and IS. In density estimation literature, it is standard to report likelihood-based metrics or density estimation error to validate that the model actually learns the target distribution rather than just producing plausible samples. The paper should include a discussion on how to evaluate the learned implicit density, such as reporting log-likelihood estimates on a held-out set or measuring density estimation error. Adding these metrics would bridge the gap between the paper's theoretical framing and its empirical validation. Readers will otherwise struggle to distinguish genuine density learning from mere visual plausibility.

Recommendation: Major revision. The core idea is strong and the empirical results are promising, but the paper contains fundamental design flaws in the class-conditional extension and lacks critical implementation details regarding training-time retrieval and discriminator memorization. Addressing these gaps is necessary to validate the method's claims and ensure reproducibility.

Revision targets:

- Restrict or explicitly analyze the class-conditional neighborhood definition to resolve the mismatch between conditioning labels and real neighbor classes.

- Provide quantitative evidence (e.g., discriminator accuracy on neighbor identification, output distribution analysis) to rule out simple memorization of small neighbor sets.

- Detail the training-time retrieval mechanism, including memory footprint, indexing strategy, and computational overhead for large datasets like ImageNet.

- Add an ablation study examining the sensitivity of generation quality to the choice of feature extractor and distance metric.

- Include an analysis of training dynamics, such as loss curves, gradient norms, or stability metrics across different neighborhood sizes and random seeds.

comments.quote:
owing equation:

\[ \begin{split}\min_{G}\max_{D}&\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_{i}\sim p(\mathbf{x}),\mathbf{x}_{n}\sim\mathcal{U}(\mathcal{A}_{i})}[\log D(\mathbf{x}_{n},f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{i}))]\\
&\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_{i}\sim p(\mathbf{x}),\mathbf{z}\sim p(\mathbf{z})}[\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z},f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{i})),f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{i})))].

comments.title: Factual inaccuracy regarding 256x256 FID results
comments.quote: In Table 3, we show that the class-conditioned IC-GAN outperforms BigGAN in terms of both FID and IS across all resolutions except the FID at  $128 \times 128$  resolution.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the data in Table 3. At 256 × 256, the standard IC-GAN (ch × 64) reports a FID of 8.3 ± 0.1, which is worse than BigGAN's 8.0 ± 0.1. The text ignores this performance gap. Correct the sentence to acknowledge the FID discrepancy at 256 × 256 or specify that the claim only applies to the augmented (ch × 64) + DA(d, i) variant.

comments.title: Invalid FID computation between mismatched dataset splits
comments.quote: We compute the FID between real data train split of the two datasets at  $128 \times 128$  resolution and obtain a score of 37.2.
comments.explanation: FID requires two sets of equal size to reliably estimate distributional distance. The ImageNet training split contains 1.28 million images, while COCO-Stuff contains roughly 17,000. The paper does not specify how these mismatched splits were subsampled or matched to compute the FID. Without this protocol, the reported score of 37.2 is methodologically unverified and cannot be compared to standard baselines. Specify the exact subsampling procedure or remove the claim.

comments.title: Contradictory train FID score for ImageNet-conditioned samples
comments.quote: When we replace the conditioning instances from COCO-Stuff with those of ImageNet, we obtain a train FID score of 43.5, underlining the important distribution shift that can be implemented by changing the conditioning instances.
comments.explanation: A train FID of 43.5 for an ImageNet-trained model conditioned on ImageNet instances is unexpectedly high. Typically, conditioning on the training distribution yields a much lower FID. This score strongly suggests an evaluation mismatch, such as comparing generated images against a COCO-Stuff ground truth set while conditioning on ImageNet instances. Clarify exactly which ground-truth reference was used for this calculation and explain why the model performs worse on its own training distribution than on out-of-domain instances.

comments.title: Overstated instance storage requirement for ImageNet
comments.quote: Experimentally, we noticed that for complex datasets, such as ImageNet, using 1,000 instances is enough to approximately cover the dataset distribution.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts the paper's own ablation results. The authors report that using 1,000 ground-truth images closest to cluster centers yields an FID of 41.8, which is substantially worse than the 10.4 FID achieved with the full method. Presenting 1,000 instances as sufficient to approximately cover a 1.2-million-image dataset misleads readers about the model's memory footprint and generation quality trade-offs. Revise the limitation section to accurately reflect the reported FID degradation at this compression ratio.

comments.title: Discriminator parameter incorrectly assigned to generator's notation
comments.quote: Therefore, our generator is jointly trained with a discriminator $D_{\theta_{G}}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{h}_{i})$ that discerns between real neighbors and generated neighbors of $\mathbf{h}_{i}$, as shown in Figure 2b.
comments.explanation: The parameter set $\theta_G$ is explicitly defined for the generator, yet it is reused for the discriminator in this sentence. This creates a notational conflict. Change $D_{\theta_{G}}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{h}_{i})$ to $D_{\theta_{D}}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{h}_{i})$ to maintain consistent notation throughout the methodology.

comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of LPIPS as diversity
comments.quote: IC-GAN exhibits notably higher LPIPS than LostGANv2 and OC-GAN, which could be explained by the fact that the latter only leverage one real sample per input conditioning during training; whereas IC-GAN uses multiple real neighboring samples per each instance, naturally favouring diversity in the generated images.
comments.explanation: The authors interpret a higher LPIPS score as evidence of greater diversity. While LPIPS measures perceptual distance, the paper does not explicitly state whether this score is computed between generated samples and their conditioning input or between multiple generated samples for the same condition. Clarify the exact computation protocol for LPIPS in this context to prevent readers from assuming the model sacrifices conditioning fidelity to achieve diversity.

comments.title: Omitted objective formulation for class-conditional extension
comments.quote: In the class-conditional case, we model  $p(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{h}_i,\mathbf{y}_j)$  with a generator  $G_{\theta_G}(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{h}_i,\mathbf{y}_j)$  trained jointly with a discriminator  $D_{\theta_D}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{h}_i,\mathbf{y}_j)$ .
comments.explanation: The section introduces a class-conditional variant but Equation (1) only specifies the objective for the unconditional case. Without the explicit minimax formulation for this extension, readers cannot verify how the expectation terms or conditioning arguments are updated. Add the corresponding class-conditional objective equation or explicitly state that Equation (1) generalizes by appending $\mathbf{y}_j$ to all conditioning inputs.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of k-means application in cited clustering GANs
comments.quote: Then, *[19, 33]* introduce an alternating two-stage approach where the first stage applies k-means to the discriminator feature space and the second stage trains a GAN conditioned on the cluster
comments.explanation: The description of [19, 33] as applying k-means specifically to the discriminator feature space is highly unusual. Standard clustering-based GAN literature typically applies k-means to data embeddings, latent spaces, or discriminator outputs. This phrasing may conflate distinct methods. Verify the exact methodology of these references and adjust the description to accurately reflect where the clustering occurs.

comments.title: Overstated architectural claim regarding per-sub-manifold generators
comments.quote: In this case, a different generator is then trained for each identified sub-manifold. By
comments.explanation: The claim that [2] trains a strictly separate generator for each identified sub-manifold appears overstated. Most self-supervised GANs utilizing k-NN graphs employ a single generator with graph-based regularization or shared weights across clusters. Training a completely independent generator per sub-manifold is computationally prohibitive for large datasets. Re-examine [2] to confirm whether it instantiates multiple generators or uses a unified architecture, and revise the text to avoid misrepresenting the baseline's complexity.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a non-parametric density estimation approach for GANs using instance conditioning and nearest neighbors. While the idea is technically sound, the manuscript contains a significant numerical contradiction in the transfer experiment results and relies heavily on pre-trained features, which undermines the novelty of the proposed architecture.

The IC-GAN method demonstrates competitive generation quality and interesting transfer capabilities, particularly in class-conditional settings. However, the paper's contribution is weakened by a discrepancy between reported experimental values and the text descriptions, as well as a lack of evaluation on density estimation metrics. The reliance on a frozen, pre-trained feature extractor also raises questions about whether the core innovation is the GAN architecture or simply the conditioning strategy on top of a strong encoder.

Overview issues:

- Discrepancies in Transfer Experiment Reporting: There is a direct contradiction between the text description of the transfer experiment and the values reported in Table 2. The text states that when conditioning an ImageNet-trained model on COCO-Stuff instances, the training FID score is 43.5. However, Table 2 reports the training FID for the 'IC-GAN (BigGAN, transf.)' model as 8.5. Furthermore, the parameter count for the transfer model (77M) is significantly higher than the base IC-GAN model (22M), yet the text implies these models are functionally the same (just with different conditioning data). This inconsistency makes it impossible to verify the reported transfer performance and undermines the confidence in the experimental results.

- Lack of Density Estimation Validation: The paper explicitly frames IC-GAN as a 'non-parametric approach to modeling distributions' and a 'mixture density estimator' (Section 1 and 3). However, the evaluation protocol relies exclusively on generation metrics (FID, IS, LPIPS) and does not include any density estimation metrics such as Negative Log Likelihood (NLL) or KL divergence. Since the paper claims to model the data density $p(x) 
eq 0$, it is essential to demonstrate that the method actually recovers the density better than standard GAN baselines or non-parametric KDE methods. Without this validation, the contribution is limited to generation quality rather than the claimed density modeling capability.

- Scalability and Inference Complexity: The IC-GAN framework requires finding the nearest neighbors of a conditioning instance for every generated sample during inference. This introduces a computational complexity of $O(N)$ per sample, where $N$ is the number of stored instances. For large-scale applications requiring millions of samples, this approach becomes prohibitively slow. The paper acknowledges storage requirements in the limitations section but completely omits any discussion of the inference time cost or scalability implications. A practical generative model must address how it handles high-throughput sampling, which this method currently does not.

- Feature Extractor Dominance in Transfer Results: The transfer experiment reveals a potential limitation in the method's architecture rather than a feature. The results show that an IC-GAN model trained on ImageNet outperforms a model trained specifically on COCO-Stuff when generating COCO-Stuff images. This suggests that the performance is primarily driven by the ImageNet pre-trained feature extractor (SwAV ResNet50) rather than the IC-GAN architecture itself. If the encoder is the dominant factor, the contribution of the GAN component is marginal. The paper should analyze whether the GAN architecture is learning meaningful density features or simply acting as a post-processing filter for the encoder's output.

Recommendation: Major Revision

Revision targets:

- Correct the numerical discrepancies in the transfer experiment section (Table 2 vs. text description) and clarify the parameter counts.

- Add a density estimation evaluation metric (e.g., NLL or KL divergence) to the experimental setup to validate the paper's core claim of modeling data density.

- Discuss the scalability of the inference pipeline and provide an analysis of the computational cost associated with neighbor lookups.

- Re-evaluate the transfer results to explain why the ImageNet-trained model outperforms the COCO-trained model, potentially by analyzing the feature extractor's role versus the GAN architecture.

comments.title: Transfer Experiment FID Discrepancy
comments.quote: When we replace the conditioning instances from COCO-Stuff with those of ImageNet, we obtain a train FID score of 43.5
comments.explanation: There is a direct contradiction between the text description of the transfer experiment and the values reported in Table 2. The text states that swapping conditioning instances from COCO-Stuff to ImageNet yields a training FID of 43.5, yet Table 2 reports the training FID for the 'IC-GAN (BigGAN, transf.)' model as 8.5. This inconsistency makes it impossible to verify the reported transfer performance.

comments.title: ImageNet-LT FID Contradiction
comments.quote: showing that IC-GAN is better in terms of FID and IS for modeling this long-tailed distribution. Note that the improvement is noticeable for each of the three groups
comments.explanation: The text claims IC-GAN is better than BigGAN for all class groups in ImageNet-LT, but Table 4 shows BigGAN actually achieves a lower FID for the 'few' class group at 256x256 resolution.

comments.title: Parameter Count Inconsistency
comments.quote:
transf.)

these approaches use tailored architectures for complex scene generation, which have at least twice the number of parameters of IC-GAN.
comments.explanation: The text claims the comparison models have 'at least twice' the parameters of IC-GAN, but Table 2 shows the transfer models have roughly 3.5x the parameters. This discrepancy undermines the comparison.

comments.title: Oversampling Hypothesis Inconsistency
comments.quote: hypothesize that oversampling some classes may result in overfitting for the discriminator, leading to low quality image generations.
comments.explanation: The authors hypothesize that oversampling causes overfitting, yet they explicitly state they used all 115k training instances without oversampling. This makes the hypothesis inconsistent with the reported setup.

comments.title: Prior Work [2] Mischaracterization
comments.quote: In this case, a different generator is then trained for each identified sub-manifold.
comments.explanation: The related work section incorrectly states that [2] trains a different generator for each sub-manifold. In reality, [2] uses a single generator conditioned on the partition index, which is a key architectural difference from IC-GAN.

comments.title: Feature Conditioning Comparison
comments.quote: this requires storing the features of all training samples.
comments.explanation: The comparison with [37] is misleading. While [37] requires storing all features, it is due to the invertible architecture, not the reconstruction objective. Furthermore, IC-GAN also requires storing features, so the claim that it 'only needs to save a very small subset' is inaccurate.

comments.title: Novelty Claim 'No Prior Work'
comments.quote: no prior work models the dataset density in a nonparametric fashion with a localized distribution around each data point with a single conditional generation network.
comments.explanation: The paper claims no prior work models density nonparametrically with a single conditional generator. However, [2] achieves a similar goal of local density modeling using a single generator conditioned on partition indices. This claim is factually incorrect.

comments.title: Mode Collapse Description
comments.quote: In turn, the generator tends to learn to generate samples covering all clusters, hence mitigating mode collapse.
comments.explanation: The description of [14] suggests the generator is trained to cover all clusters to mitigate mode collapse. In reality, [14] focuses on density estimation in the discriminator; the generator is not explicitly trained to cover clusters.

comments.title: Long-tail Claim 'No Prior Work'
comments.quote: However, to the best of our knowledge, no other previous work tackles this problem for GANs.
comments.explanation: The paper claims no other work tackles long-tail distributions in GANs. However, the cited baseline [8] explicitly addresses this problem, even if it struggles with it. This claim contradicts the premise of the comparison.

comments.title: Transfer Capability Claim
comments.quote: obtaining competitive results on ImageNet and surpassing the BigGAN baseline on the challenging ImageNet-LT
comments.explanation: The discussion claims IC-GAN 'surpasses' BigGAN on ImageNet-LT, but this relies on generation metrics (FID/IS) which do not validate density modeling. Without density estimation metrics, this claim is unsubstantiated.

comments.title: Scalability Omission
comments.quote: First, as kernel density estimator approaches, IC-GAN requires storing training instances to use the model.
comments.explanation: The limitations section correctly notes the storage requirement but omits the computational cost of finding nearest neighbors for every sample during inference. This O(N) complexity is a significant practical limitation that should be addressed.

comments.title: Transfer Limitation Vagueness
comments.quote: in the case of transfer to datasets that are *very* different from ImageNet, the quality of generated images degrades.
comments.explanation: The limitation states quality degrades for datasets 'very different' from ImageNet, but this is vague. It is unclear what constitutes 'very different' or how the degradation compares to the feature extractor's performance.
